content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES CoMMIsSION
September 12, 1997
Jim Shields
Shields Political Consulting Services
P. O.Box 490
Laytonville, CA 95454
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-97-437
Dear Mr. Shields:
This letter is in response to your request for informal assistance regarding the provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’ In a telephone conversation on September 12, 1997,
you stated that you are requesting this information on behalf of John Pinches who is the 3’”
District Supervisor in Mendocino County and who has also announced his candidacy for the
Senate District.
QUESTION
Do the “blackout” provisions of Section 85305 prohibit Mr. Pinches from loaning andlor
making unlimited contributions from his personal funds to his own campaign during the
“blackout” period?
CONCLUSION
No. The “blackout” provisions of Section 85305 do not prohibit Mr. Pinches from
loaning andlor making unlimited contributions of his personal funds to his own campaign during
the blackout period provided such loans and contributions are made in accordance with the
provisions of Section 85307, as well as in accordance with all other applicable campaign finance
reporting and disclosure laws.
FACTS
You have provided no facts.
Government Code sections 81000
- 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No. A-97-437
Page 2
ANALYSIS
The contribution limitations, including the “blackout” period, within Proposition 208 do
not apply to a candidate’s contributions ofhis or her personal funds to his or her campaign
committee. (Section 85301(e); Moll Advice Letter, No. 1-97-013; Sutton Advice Letter, No.
1-97-130; Edmonds Advice Letter, No. A-97-137; Olson Advice Letter, No. A-97-229.)
Section 85301 ofthe Act provides that the contribution limitations ofProposition 208
“shall not apply to a candidate’s contribution of his or her personal funds to his or her own
campaign committee, but shall apply to contributions from a spouse.” (Section 8530 1(e).)
2
Section 84216 provides that “a loan received by a candidate or committee is a contribution ....“
It follows that any loan of personal funds by an officeholder to his or her campaign account is
likewise not subject to the contribution limits ofProposition 208. Of course, any loan by the
candidate to his or her campaign account must be made in accordance with the other provisions
ofthe Act. Section 85307(c) allows a candidate to contribute to his or her campaign committee
in the form of a loan which does not total more than $20,000 at any one point in time.
3 (Burgess
Advice Letter, No. 1-97-022.) Therefore, a candidate cannot have an outstanding loan balance of
more than $20,000, even if it is a personal loan.
Ifyou have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Lynda Doherty
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division
SGC:LLD :jlw
2 Consistent with this policy, we have previously concluded that an officeholder may contribute personal
funds in unlimited amounts to his or her own officeholder account. (Markman Advice Letter, No. A-97-235.)
The candidate’s contribution may come from conununity property funds. If the candidate signs the
check from a community property account to contribute money to his or her campaign account, the candidate is
considered the contributor (Regulation 18533). (Burgess Advice Letter, supra.)
